name: Meandro de Chodes y Morata de Jalón — circular by the Jalón river
name_original: Meandro de Chodes y Morata de Jalón — circular por el río Jalón
region_intro: Hiking around Chodes offers diverse landscapes within the Zaragoza province of Aragon, Spain. The region is characterized by the Jalón River, which carves picturesque meanders through rocky formations and riverbanks shaded by trees. Surrounding areas feature varied ecosystems, including limestone gorges, semi-arid steppes, and extensive pine forests, providing a range of hiking experiences. The terrain includes riverine paths, moderate ascents to viewpoints, and more challenging routes through hills. Best hiking trails around Chodes The most popular hiking route is Meandro de Chodes y Morata de Jalón — circular by the Jalón river, an easy 4.4 miles (7.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 57 minutes to complete. This route follows the scenic meander of the Jalón River, winding between rocky formations. Another top favourite among local hikers is Mirador Cruz de Alberto loop from La Almunia de Doña Godina, a moderate 7.9 miles (12.7 km) path. This trail offers varied terrain with ascents to viewpoints overlooking the landscape. Local hikers also love the Pico Cabrera from Sestrica — circular via the Sierra de la Virgen, a 11.9 miles (19.2 km) trail leading through the Sierra de la Virgen, often completed in about 6 hours 21 minutes. Hiking around Chodes is defined by riverine paths along the Jalón, rocky formations, and hills offering panoramic views. The network of trails offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside walks to more challenging ascents. The routes in Chodes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 80 hikers have used komoot to explore Chodes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Chodes?
A: There are over 20 hiking routes available around Chodes, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. These include easy riverside walks, moderate ascents to viewpoints, and more challenging routes through the hills.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes near Chodes?
A: Hikes near Chodes primarily feature riverine paths along the picturesque Jalón River, winding between rocky formations and shaded riverbanks. The wider Zaragoza province also offers diverse Aragonese landscapes, including limestone gorges, semi-arid steppes, and extensive pine forests, providing varied terrain from gentle riverside strolls to more challenging ascents.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the Chodes area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. A popular easy option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979525564/meandro-de-chodes-y-morata-de-jalon-circular-by-the-jalon-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Meandro de Chodes y Morata de Jalón — circular by the Jalón river&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the scenic meander of the Jalón. For a more challenging experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979996918/pico-cabrera-from-sestrica-circular-via-the-sierra-de-la-virgen" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Cabrera from Sestrica — circular via the Sierra de la Virgen&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while hiking around Chodes?
A: Along the trails, you can enjoy the scenic Jalón River meander and its rocky formations. Notable nearby attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5284780" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Capurnos Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4822549" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ridge of the Sierra de Algairén&lt;/a&gt;, and a &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5793858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Scenic Road Through a Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. The wider Zaragoza province also boasts the stunning waterfalls of Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park and the impressive Moncayo Summit.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options near Chodes?
A: Yes, the Chodes area offers family-friendly options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979525564/meandro-de-chodes-y-morata-de-jalon-circular-by-the-jalon-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Meandro de Chodes y Morata de Jalón — circular by the Jalón river&lt;/a&gt; is an easy, picturesque route suitable for families. Generally, riverine paths along the Jalón are less strenuous and offer pleasant walks for all ages.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the trails around Chodes?
A: Many trails in the Chodes area are suitable for dogs, especially the riverine paths. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near agricultural land, and to check for any specific local regulations or protected areas that might have restrictions.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Chodes?
A: The Zaragoza province, where Chodes is located, experiences hot summers and cold winters. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant hiking conditions with milder temperatures. These seasons are ideal for enjoying the diverse landscapes without extreme heat or cold.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979996918/pico-cabrera-from-sestrica-circular-via-the-sierra-de-la-virgen" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Cabrera from Sestrica — circular via the Sierra de la Virgen&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 11.9-mile trail with significant elevation gain through the Sierra de la Virgen, often taking over six hours to complete.

Q: How can I get to the hiking trails around Chodes using public transport?
A: While specific public transport options directly to trailheads in Chodes might be limited, the municipality is accessible by road. For detailed public transport information, it's best to check local bus schedules from larger towns in the Zaragoza province, such as La Almunia de Doña Godina, which is a starting point for some routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31456282" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador Cruz de Alberto loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads in Chodes?
A: Parking availability can vary by trailhead. For popular routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979525564/meandro-de-chodes-y-morata-de-jalon-circular-by-the-jalon-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Meandro de Chodes y Morata de Jalón&lt;/a&gt;, you can typically find parking in or near the villages of Chodes or Morata de Jalón. For more remote trails, it's advisable to research specific starting points beforehand.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Chodes?
A: The trails in Chodes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 20 reviews. Hikers often praise the picturesque riverine paths, the unique rocky formations, and the varied landscapes that offer both easy strolls and more challenging ascents.

Q: Are there any notable natural parks or reserves in the wider Zaragoza province that offer hiking?
A: Absolutely. Within the wider Zaragoza province, you can explore the renowned Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park, famous for its stunning waterfalls and lush landscapes. The Aragonese Pyrenees also host the Western Valleys Natural Park, featuring extensive forests, mountain peaks, and high mountain lakes, offering diverse hiking and wildlife viewing opportunities.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while hiking in the region?
A: The diverse ecosystems of the Zaragoza province support various wildlife. Along the Jalón River, you might spot various bird species, including birds of prey that frequent the calcareous walls. In the broader Aragonese Pyrenees, within parks like the Western Valleys, there's a chance to see larger mammals like brown bears and impressive birds such as bearded vultures.
tips:
We are on the Capurnos bridge located in a beautiful natural environment that connects the towns of Morata de Jalón and Chodes. Near the bridge there is a recreational area with the possibility of doing an initiation ferrata
Nearby is also one of the most emblematic climbing schools in Zaragoza known as Torcas de Chodes. Within its walls there are more than 600 routes for all levels.

We are in Plaza España in the town of Morata de Jalón, a wonderful historical place that is undoubtedly worth a walk. We will see the imposing church of Santa Ana and the Renaissance-Baroque palace (now town hall).
From this point, with the river Jalón in the middle, we will have good views of the ruins of the Castle of Chodes, abandoned in the 17th century and located on the Peña de Lodos.

waypoints.name: Plaza de España and Church of Santa Ana, Morata de Jalón
